coredata.dc:title: Insights into the interaction of human serum albumin and carbon dots: Hydrothermal synthesis and biophysical study
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Chun-Yan
Pan, Juan
Bai, Ai-Min
Hu, Yan-Jun
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) have been widely studied with high quantum yield (QY). The experimental conditions of three different N,S-CDs were optimized. Emission peak position of three different N,S-CDs shown almost remains unchanged or obvious excitation-dependent PL properties, that was likely owed to size distribution. In order to discuss the N,S-CDs stability of photoluminescence property in environment, various experiments such as the photostability, different pH, ionic strengths and temperature were designed. To sum up, three different N,S-CDs exhibited discrepancy property. Molecular interaction of three different N,S-CDs were produced via vary carbon source with human serum albumins have been investigate by various methods. The quenching mechanism, thermodynamic and kinetic parameters, binding sites, electrochemical behavior of three different N,S-CDs with human serum albumins have some different, but conformational change of three different N,S-CDs with human serum albumins alike. The molecular docking had successful applied to study the N,S-CDs interaction with HSA. Different N,S-CDs possessed various characteristic that will have different quenching mechanism when they interaction with human serum albumin, study the mechanism of action at molecular level will help people to choose suitable CDs to apply in nanomedical.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Human serum albumin
Doped carbon dots
Interaction mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Probe different N,S-CDs with HSA interaction
Site-selective binding of CDs (AA) to HSA
Conformational changes of HSA with the presence of different N,S-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Foundation for Excellent Creative Research Group of Hubei Provincial Department of Education
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21273065 ), and the Science and Technology Foundation for Excellent Creative Research Group of Hubei Provincial Department of Education ( T201810 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A–C) UV–vis absorption spectrum and excitation spectrum as well as emission spectrum of CDs (CA), CDs (glucose) and CDs (AA), respectively; (D) Fluorescence spectra recorded from 310 nm to 390 nm with 10 nm increment of CDs (CA); (E) Fluorescence spectra of CDs (glucose) recorded from 320 nm to 400 nm with 10 nm increment; (F) Fluorescence spectra of CDs (AA) recorded from 310 nm to 420 nm with 10 nm increment.

Representation of three different N,S-CDs. (A), (C) and (E) represented the typical TEM and the inset shown is size distribution of CDs (CA), CDs (glucose) and CDs (AA), respectively. (B), (D) and (F) indicated the lattice parameters of CDs (CA), CDs (glucose) and CDs (AA), respectively.
(A–E) XPS full spectra, XPS spectra of S2p, XPS spectra of C1s, XPS spectra of N1s and XPS spectra of O1s for the as-prepare CDs (CA), respectively. (F) FTIR spectrum of the as-synthesized CDs (CA).

Fluorescence emission spectra of HSA with (A) CDs (CA), (B) CDs (glucose) and (C) CDs (AA) respectively added; c(HSA) = 5.0 × 10−6 mol·L−1, c(CDs (CA))/10−3 mol·L−1, a–i: from 0.0 to 4.0 at increments of 0.5; c(CDs (glucose))/10−4 mol·L−1, a–i: from 0.0 to 4.0 at increments of 0.5; c(CDs (AA))/10−5 mol·L−1, a-i: from 0.0 to 8.0 at increments of 1.0.

(A) Molecular docking of HSA-CDs (AA) interaction bind on site I; (B) the part map of residues interaction with CDs (AA) on site I; (C) Amino acid residues form hydrogen bond with CDs (AA); (D) CDs (AA) was at the bind pocket.

(A–B) Cyclic voltammetry and Nyquist plots of CDs (CA)/Au electrode with various concentrations of HSA, respectively. (C–D) Cyclic voltammetry and Nyquist plots of HSA/Au electrode with various concentrations of CDs (glucose), respectively. (E–F) Cyclic voltammetry and Nyquist plots of HSA/Au electrode with various concentrations of CDs (AA), respectively.

(A–C) Synchronous fluorescence spectrum of HSA-CDs (CA), HSA-CDs (glucose) and HSA-CDs (AA) system, respectively; (D-F) CD spectrum of HSA-CDs (CA), HSA-CDs (glucose) and HSA-CDs (AA) system, respectively.
The three-dimension fluorescence spectra of (A) HSA, (B) HSA-CDs (CA), (C) HSA-CDs (CA) and (D) HSA-CDs (CA) (D), respectively.
Summary of fluorescence lifetimes of CDs (CA), CDs (glucose) and CDs (AA).
Stern-Volmer quenching constants at different temperatures and relative activation energy of HSA-CDs (CA) system and HSA-CDs (glucose) system.
Stern-Volmer quenching constants, Binding constants, binding sites and relative thermodynamic parameters of HSA-CDs (AA) system.
Bind constant of site marker of HSA-CDs system.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: R is the correlation coefficient b S.D. is the standard deviation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Relevant parameters of the three-dimension fluorescence spectra of HSA with different N,S-CDs.

head.ce:title: Insights into the interaction of human serum albumin and carbon dots: Hydrothermal synthesis and biophysical study

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis & Reuse Technology, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei Normal University, Huangshi 435002, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis & Reuse Technology
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis & Reuse Technology, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei Normal University, Huangshi 435002, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) have been widely studied with high quantum yield (QY). The experimental conditions of three different N,S-CDs were optimized. Emission peak position of three different N,S-CDs shown almost remains unchanged or obvious excitation-dependent PL properties, that was likely owed to size distribution. In order to discuss the N,S-CDs stability of photoluminescence property in environment, various experiments such as the photostability, different pH, ionic strengths and temperature were designed. To sum up, three different N,S-CDs exhibited discrepancy property. Molecular interaction of three different N,S-CDs were produced via vary carbon source with human serum albumins have been investigate by various methods. The quenching mechanism, thermodynamic and kinetic parameters, binding sites, electrochemical behavior of three different N,S-CDs with human serum albumins have some different, but conformational change of three different N,S-CDs with human serum albumins alike. The molecular docking had successful applied to study the N,S-CDs interaction with HSA. Different N,S-CDs possessed various characteristic that will have different quenching mechanism when they interaction with human serum albumin, study the mechanism of action at molecular level will help people to choose suitable CDs to apply in nanomedical.

ce:section.ce:para: Among carbon-base nanomaterials carbon dots (CDs) have attracted considerable attention. Compared with polymers, conventional organic dyes, metal nanoparticles and semiconductor quantum dots, CDs possess numerous superior property such as low toxicity, good biocompatibility, environment-friendly, ease of functionalization, highly water-dispersible, easy preparation as well as photostability [1–4]. Thus, CDs have presented great potential in various applications, including biomedicine, bioimaging, biosensing, optoelectronic devices, fluorescent probes, et al. [2,5–8]. However, there are some urgent questions to solve. For instance, most of CDs have a relatively low quantum yield, a short-wavelength blue-green region of CDs distinctly confine CDs application in biologically-relevant fields. Different method including heteroatom doping, surface functional and surface passivation were used to improve the property of CDs. Heteroatom doping include nitrogen, sulfur, fluorine, phosphorus and boron, etc. It through improving optical and electronic properties, improving affinity and increasing the quantum yield of CDs. Nitrogen (N) atom can combine strongly with the C atom due to the size of it being similar to C; S doping can tune the maximum fluorescence emission of CDs to a longer wavelength, and improve the fluorescence intensity of CDs. So, nitrogen and sulfur heteroatom doping are used to prepare CDs in this study [9–13].

Serum albumins are the most plentiful protein of blood plasma, that consists of three homologous domains, each domain is divided into two subdomains. Human serum albumins (HSA) are used widely as a biological model to study the molecular interaction with nanoparticle, which can carriage, allocation, and pass on many endogenous and exogenous substances [14–16]. To date, CDs is appeal to tremendous biomedical applications, an increasing number of researchers to probe the molecular interaction in between biomacromolecule and CDs [7,15]. Drug interaction with proteins can be modulated the bioavailability of drug, variation of binding constants of three drugs with HSA and γ-globulins with different concentration of CDs were discussed [17]. The quenching constants, thermodynamic parameters, binding location and conformation changes between CDs with different serum albumins were explored [18,19]. CDs as a fluorescent probe to selective bovine serum albumin and DNA were investigated [20,21]. Not only different optical properties, discrepancy particle sizes but also surface chemical properties of obtained CDs by discrepancy synthetic methods and raw materials, thus, there are different effect on protein structure and function via interaction with each kind of CDs [22].

In this research, three different N,S-CDs were produced via vary carbon source, interaction with human serum albumin have been discussed. To our knowledge, system compare the influence of different carbon source to synthesis N,S-CDs and compare different N,S-CDs interaction with human serum albumin is rarely to report. The molecular docking had successful applied to study the N,S-CDs interaction with HSA. Discrepancy quantum yield and property of N,S-CDs were produced. Different N,S-CDs possessed various characteristic that will have different quenching mechanism when they interaction with human serum albumin, study the mechanism of action at molecular level will help people to choose suitable CDs to apply in nanomedical.

Ascorbic acid (AA) and N-Acetyl-L-cysteine (NAC) were purchase from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent. HSA and hydroxymethyl aminomethanne (Tris) were purchase from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA); ibuprofen and warfarin were purchase from Hubei Biocause Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. (Hubei, China; the purity no less than 99.7%) and Trust Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China; the purity no less than 99.5%), respectively; Every other reagents used everywhere this research were analytical purity. Ultrapure water was prepared using deionized water (18.25 MΩ cm−1) and the ultrapurification system EPED-E2-20TJ (Nanjing EPED technology development Co. Ltd., China).

N,S-CDs represent by nitrogen and sulfur heteroatom doping synthesis. Different N,S-CDs were prepared through a hydrothermal method [11] using citric acid (CA), glucose and ascorbic acid (AA) as various carbon source, CA is popular in the preparation of CDs, glucose plays an important role in biology, AA is one of the most important vitamins. Carbon source low consumption and no poison [23]. Urea and N-Acetyl-L-cysteine are as nitrogen and sulfur doping agent. 0.96 g CA, 0.30 g urea and 0.40 g NAC, 0.90 g glucose, 0.30 g urea and 0.40 g NAC, 0.88 g AA, 0.30 g urea and 0.40 g NAC were dissolved into 20 mL deionized water respectively, with stirred for 10 min at room temperature. Three mixed solution were transferred into three 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene autoclaves for heating 1.5 h at 160 °C. Obtained products were purified through a 0.22 μm filter membrane that put together for next step applications. The concentration of the different CDs was measured on the basis of the mean molecular weight as determined by MALDI-TOF-MS [15,17,24]. The results is shown in Fig. 1S, for convenience, according to the different carbon source, the three different N,S-CDs was named CDs (CA), CDs (glucose) and CDs (AA), the measured molar concentration of CDs (CA), CDs (glucose) and CDs (AA) were 993.87, 634.30 and 1329.43 respectively.

The quinine sulfate always used as a reference to figure up the quantum yield of CDs. Quantum yield of three different CDs was used to calculate by the following equation:
(1)
Φ X
=
Φ ST
×
m X
m ST
×
η 2
X
η 2
ST

Here, Φ is the quantum yield, m is slope of Fluorescence and absorbance of CDs or quinine sulfate. Quinine sulfate (Φ ST = 0.54) used 0.1 mol∙L−1 H2SO4 (refractive index η
ST = 1.33) to dissolve and CDs used deionized water (η
X = 1.33) to dissolve. X is the sample and ST is the standard. The absorption in the 10 mm fluorescence cuvette should be retained below 0.1 to minimize re-absorption effects. 360 nm was chosen as the excitation wavelength of sample and quinine sulfate, excitation and emission slit width of sample and quinine sulfate were used 10 nm and 7.5 nm, wavelength range of fluorescence spectra were recorded within 370 nm to 600 nm.

Fluorescence spectra at three temperatures (298, 304, and 310 K) were recorded. 10.0 nm was chosen as the slit widths of excitation and emission. Trace syringes were manually used to performed titrations. FTIR spectroscopy was recorded in Thermo Nicolet iS5 iD5 by total reflection. The CD spectra were carried out at room temperature and under sustained nitrogen protection, testing range from 200 to 240 nm with scanning speed for 50 nm∙min−1. The buffer solution Tris-HCl always used to revise each spectrum.

The electrochemical behavior of HSA with different CDs was investigated by measuring the cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). A three-electrode system was used, the scan range of CV was from −0.2 to 0.8, a scan rate of CV was 0.05 V s−1and the sensitivity was 10−4. Frequency range of EIS was from 100,000 to 0.1 Hz, EIS with a DC potential was +0.32 V and EIS with AC amplitude was 5 mV. The electrochemical cell was equipped with electrolyte which containing 5 × 10−3 mol∙L−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− and 0.1 mol∙L−1 KCl. 5 × 10−6 mol∙L−1 was chosen as the concentration of HSA. 6 × 10−2 mol∙L−1 CDs (CA) solution got 10 μL to titrate to the Au electrode, subsequently this system was silenced at room temperature for 12 h. Electrolyte added to different concentrations of the HSA solution, the electrolyte solution was stirred for 1 min, and the CDs/Au electrode system was taken into electrolyte to incubate for 4 min before carrying out the experimental test. The polished Au electrode was dipped in 5 mL 5 × 10−6 mol∙L−1 HSA for 24 h, and then Electrolyte added to CDs (glucose) or CDs (AA), the electrolyte solution was stirred for 1 min, and taken the CDs/Au electrode system into electrolyte to incubate for 4 min before carrying out the experimental test respectively.

A Surflex Dock program in the Sybyl-X 2.1.1 package was investigated the HSA interaction with CDs via molecular docking. Before the docking study, added hydrogen atoms, surface charge of biopolymer was used AMBER7 FF99 method, some ligand was deleted that to produce the protocol and every water molecules must be removed. The molecular conformation optimize of CDs (AA) was used a Tripos Force Field. Charge distribution of CDs (AA) was determined with Gasteiger-Huckel method.

The different reaction times and the various doping agents can affect the QY of N,S-CDs. The QY of CDs (CA) reaction time for 1 h, 1.5 h, 2 h, 2.5 h and 3 h was had be discussed. As illustrated in Fig. 2S(A), the QY were 38.70%, 68.50%, 51.70%, 37.20% and 35.90% for the reaction time 1 h, 1.5 h, 2 h, 2.5 h and 3 h of CDs (CA), respectively. As shown in Fig. 2S(B), the QY of CA add with urea or CA add with NAC are 17.00% and 43.30%, respectively. The experimental conditions were optimized as follows: 0.96 g CA, 0.30 g urea and 0.40 g NAC were dissolved into 20 mL deionized water for heating 1.5 h at 160 °C. The QY of CDs (glucose) and CDs (AA) are 0.49% and 0.74%, respectively, at heating 1.5 h for 160 °C.

Intrinsic property of CDs can be obtained by fluorescence lifetime. Influence factors of fluorescence lifetime are may be largely rest with the intrinsic of the fluorescence sites and environment [21]. Data of fluorescence lifetime of three N,S-CDs were analyzed by the follow equation:
(2)
τ average
=
Α 1
τ 1
2
+
Α 2
τ 2
2
/
Α 1
τ 1
+
Α 2
τ 2

Here, A 1 and A 2 represent the fractional contributions of the time-resolved decay lifetime of τ 1 and τ 2. Three different carbon dots with fluorescence lifetime were measured by time-resolved fluorescence, as show in Fig. 3S and Table 1 , the fluorescence lifetime of CDs (CA), CDs (glucose) and CDs (AA) are 9.90 ns, 7.37 ns and 6.59 ns.

Fig. 1(A–C) shows that was the UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of three different N,S-CDs in aqueous solution. Fig. 1A displays a UV–vis characteristic absorption peak at 341 nm, it assigned to the capture of excited carriers via the localized apparent states, and maximum excitation and emission wavelength of CDs (CA) show at 345 nm and 421 nm, respectively. The CDs (glucose) UV–vis characteristic absorption peaks at 271 nm show in Fig. 1B, which possible owned to the n-π* transition of CO band and its maximum excitation and emission wavelength characterized at 366 nm and 451 nm. As exhibited in Fig. 1C, the CDs (AA) possess an UV–vis characteristic absorption peaks at 265 nm ascribes to π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains, maximum excitation wavelength is 373 nm, meanwhile maximum emission wavelength is 453 nm. As show in Fig. 1(D–F), emission wavelength of three N,S-CDs are rely on excitation wavelength . Emission peak position almost remains unchanged, was likely to owe to the apparent states of CDs (CA) and the uniformity of size distribution. CDs (glucose) and CDs (AA) show excitation-dependent PL, which may be attributed to the wide size distribution and various emissive trap sites [23,25].

In order to discuss the N,S-CDs stability of photoluminescence property in environment, the pH effect of different carbon dots were exhibited in Fig. 4S(A–C). The fluorescence intensity of CDs (CA) with the pH from 7.2 to12.2 shows a steady tendency, however, when pH values increased from 2.2 to 7.2, the fluorescence intensity increased and showed blue-shift with the change of pH values. Fluorescence intensity of CDs (glucose) was no change over a wide pH values range from 4.2 to 10.2, but when the pH value no matter at meta-acid or leaning alkalinity, it were shown sensitive. The fluorescence intensity of CDs (AA) keep invariability when the pH values from 2.2 to 10.2, and the fluorescence intensity were decreased at pH values for the 12.2. The fluorescence intensity changes of three different carbon dots are varied because of the different carbon source. As exhibited in Fig. 4S(D), fluorescence intensity of CDs (CA), CDs (glucose) and CDs (AA) keep nearly invariability with different concentrations of NaCl in the aqueous solution. The stable fluorescence emission with the wide range of pH and ionic strengths is very significant for analytical application. From inspection of Fig. 4S(E), the fluorescence intensity of three different carbon dots are decreased with the temperature changed from 5 °C to 85 °C, this indicates that those carbon dots are temperature-dependent. The photostability of the three different carbon dots were probed exhibited in Fig. 4S(F). Fluorescence intensity of three different carbon dots had tiny variation with continuous shine under a 150 W Xe lamp for 50 min, and that imply the excellent photostability of three different carbon dots [26,27].

The morphology and structure of the three different N,S-CDs were investigated via TEM. From inspection of Fig. 2(A, C and E), average diameter of CDs (CA), CDs (glucose) and CDs (AA) are measured with 2.0 nm, 7.6 nm and 13.9 nm, respectively. And the dispersity of CDs (CA) is good, others dispersity of N,S-CDs is a little bad. Fig. 2(B, D and F) is shown that the lattice parameters of CDs (CA), CDs (glucose) and CDs (AA), respectively. The lattice spacing of CDs (CA) is 0.20 nm, which is likely due to graphitic carbon that the (100) facet. Lattice parameters of CDs (glucose) is 0.32 nm which may be corresponding graphitic carbon that the (002) diffraction plane. Interplanar spacing of CDs (AA) is 0.28 nm which indicated good crystallinity of CDs (AA), and the value may be attributed to graphitic carbon that the (020) plane [28–30]. Those three different N,S-CDs were synthesis under same condition, the diversity of morphology, size distribution and lattice parameters may be because of the different carbon sources.

The chemical compositions of three different N,S-CDs were characterized by XPS. All N,S-CDs displays four dominant peaks, from inspection of Fig. 3A, characteristic peaks of CDs (CA) show at 163.55 eV, 285.09 eV, 400.10 eV, 532.29 eV owed to S2p, C1s, N1s and O1s. Four peaks of CDs (glucose) shown in Fig. 4S(A) at 163.29 eV, 285.04 eV, 399.54 eV and 531.75 eV represent S2p, C1s, N1s and O1s. From Fig. 6S(A) can find the peaks of CDs (AA) at 163.33 eV, 284.76 eV, 400.15 eV and 531.86 eV represent the S2p, C1s, N1s and O1s. From inspection of Fig. 3(B–E), The S2p of CDs (CA) shown two components assigned to S2p3/2 and S2p3/2 of thiophene S at 163.2 eV and 164.3 eV, respectively, the C1s of CDs (CA) deconvoluted into six components at 284.5 eV, 285.3 eV, 286.0 eV, 286.5 eV, 288.2 eV and 289.0 eV assigned to C-C/C=C, CS,C-N, CO, C=O/C=N and –COOH, respectively, the N1s of CDs (CA) exhibited three main peaks at 399.5 eV, 400.4 eV and 401.6 eV, that attribute to pyridinic C-N-C, pyrrolic C2-N-H and graphitic NC3, respectively, the O1s spectrum of CDs (CA) display three peaks at 531.3 eV, 532.5 eV and 533.6 eV, which were associated with CO, C-O-H and O=C-O, respectively. From inspection of Fig. 5S(B–E), the S2p and C1s of CDs (glucose) deconvoluted peaks were same with the CDs (CA), the O1s of CDs (glucose) mainly consists of two peaks at 531.6 eV and 533.3 eV ascribing to CO and CO bonds, respectively, the N1s of CDs (glucose) reveals two fitted peaks at 399.4 eV and 400.9 eV suggest attribute to pyridinic C-N-C and graphitic N-(C)3, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6S(B–E), the S2p and N1s of CDs (AA) fitted peaks were agree with the CDs (CA), the O1s of CDs (AA) consistent with the CDs (glucose) fitted peaks, the peak at 283.99 eV, 284.82 eV, 285.80 eV and 288.21 eV in C1s spectrum demonstrates the presence of CC, C-O/C-S, CN and CO, respectively [26,30–32].

The FTIR spectrum of those three N,S-CDs were shown, From inspection of Fig. 3F, CDs (CA) FTIR spectrum, the peak located in 3439 cm−1 and 3164 cm−1 were suggested OH and NH that stretching vibrations, peak located in 1620 cm−1 indicates that presence CN and CO that stretching vibrations, peak located in 1404 cm−1 were suggested owed to –COOH group, the stretching vibrations of CO and CS were emerged at 1226 cm−1, 1132 cm−1, respectively. From inspection of Fig. 5S(F) is CDs (glucose) FTIR spectrum, the peak at 3442 cm−1 and 3251 cm−1 were suggested owed to the OH and NH that stretching vibrations, 1634 cm−1 indicates presence stretching vibrations of CN and CO, peak at 1399 cm−1 represent the –COOH group, stretching vibrations of CO was located in 1080 cm−1, respectively. The CDs (AA) FTIR spectrum was shown in Fig. 6S(F), the peak at 3442 cm−1 and 3224 cm−1 were suggested owed to the OH and NH that its stretching vibrations, peak was located in 1632 cm−1 suggested that presence CN and CO which stretching vibrations, peak at 1399 cm−1 was attributed to –COOH group, the CS and CO that stretching vibrations were appeared at 1112 cm−1, 1038 cm−1, respectively [1,2,33,34]. The XPS and FTIR want to tell people to know that diversity property of different N,S-CDs by different carbon source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Probe different N,S-CDs with HSA interaction

ce:section.ce:para: Steady-state fluorescence spectrometry generally used to study the interaction of nanoparticles with biomacromolecules, to obtain the part micro-environmental information of biomacromolecules [15]. In this research, kept concentrations of HSA solution remain no change at 5.0 × 10−6 mol·L−1, concentrations of CDs (CA) changed from 0 to 4.0 × 10−3 mol·L−1, concentrations of CDs (glucose) changed from 0 to 4.0 × 10−4 mol·L−1 and the concentrations of CDs (AA) also changed from 0 to 8.0 × 10−5 mol·L−1, this phenomenon illustrate that CDs (AA) exhibited a strong quenching capacity on the intrinsic fluorescence of HSA. Fluorescence spectra of HSA interaction with different N,S-CDs at 298 K is shown in Fig. 4 . In order to avoid the fluorescence peak of CDs (CA), HSA chose excitation wavelength at 275 nm. The intrinsic fluorescence that maximum emission wavelength of HSA was decrease with the concentrations change by different N,S-CDs.

As well know that quenching mechanism including static quenching and dynamic quenching, so that to obtain quenching mechanism between three different N,S-CDs and HSA, various temperatures have been investigated be established in their rely on temperature. As everyone knows that static quenching is a process to generate a ground-state fluorophore-quencher complex. However, dynamic quenching also name collisional quenching which depend on diffusion process, diffusion is a solvent-rely process that lesser viscosity when increase in temperature lead to rapid diffusion [35].

Data of fluorescence quenching were analyzed by the famous Stern-Volmer equation [36]:
(3)
F 0
F
= 1 + K sv
Q
where F 0 and F are the steady-state fluorescence intensities of HSA in the absence and presence of different N,S-CDs, respectively. K
SV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, [Q] represents the concentration of different N,S-CDs. According to Eq. (3), the K
SV was calculated at different temperatures. From inspection of Table 2 , K
SV values of HSA-CDs (CA) and HSA-CDs (glucose) system were increased as the temperature increased, which indicated that the quenching mechanism of those two systems were dynamic quenching. But as shown in Table 3 , the K
SV values of HSA-CDs (AA) system is inversely according with temperature, that imply the quenching mechanism between this system was static quenching. The different quenching mechanism between various N,S-CDs with HSA may be because synthesis carbon dots using different carbon source, the discrepancy functional group of carbon source due to the distinction N,S-CDs.

The dynamic quenching depends on diffusion process which can be calculated using the activation energy. The activation energy for the dynamic quenching used the Arrhenius equation to evaluate [37]:
(4)
ln
K SV
= −
E a
RT
+ ln A
where K
SV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant at relevant temperature, E a is activation energy for the dynamic quenching, R represent the gas constant and the parameter A is denoted the frequency factor or pre-exponential factor. The Arrhenius plot of CDs (CA) and CDs (glucose) exhibited in Fig. 7S(A–B), and the calculate value exhibited in Table 2.

Data of static quenching also used modified Stern-Volmer equation to calculation [35]:
(5)
F 0
ΔF
=
1
f a
K a
1
Q
+
1
f a
where ΔF represents the difference fluorescence that absence and presence of the CDs (AA) at concentration [Q], f a is the fraction of accessible fluorescence and K a is the effective quenching constant for the accessible HSA. Plots of modified Stern-Volmer exhibited in Fig. 7S(C), and relevant results are shown in Table 3. We found that K a decreased when temperature increased, which same with K
SV change trend by temperature, that further confirm quenching mechanism of HSA-CDs (AA) system were static quenching.

According Double Logarithm equation can be analyzed binding constants of system [38]:
(6)
log
F 0
− F
F
= log
K b
+ n log
Q
where F 0 and F represent the steady-state fluorescence intensities of HSA in the absence and presence of CDs (AA), respectively. K b represents the binding constant and n is binding stoichiometry, [Q] represents the concentration of CDs (AA). Double Logarithm plots shown in Fig. 7S(D), and the calculate value shown in Table 3. It exhibits that downtrend of binding constant K b of HSA-CDs (AA) system with the increasing temperature, illustrate the binding capacity of CDs (AA) to HSA was reduced. The n value was approximately equal to 1 which indicating there was only a single binding site of CDs (AA) on HSA.

Interaction forces between nanoparticle and biomolecules include van der Waals forces, hydrophobic interaction, electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonds, and so on. At studied temperature, if enthalpy change (ΔH) slight change, it is can be used the van't Hoff equation to calculate both enthalpy change (ΔH) and entropy change (ΔS) [35]:
(7)
ln K = −
ΔH RT
+
ΔS R
where K represents analog to binding constant at relevant temperature and R represent the gas constant. From the following relationship can be evaluated free energy change (ΔG):
(8)
ΔG = ΔH − TΔS

Fig. 7S(E) shows van't Hoff plot, and the enthalpy change (ΔH) obtain from van't Hoff slope. Table 3 displays thermodynamic parameters for the HSA-CDs (AA) system. The negative ΔH and the positive ΔS value generally demonstrate electrostatic forces present at the reaction process between CDs (AA) and HSA. The negative ΔG value represent binding process is spontaneous [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Site-selective binding of CDs (AA) to HSA
ce:section.ce:para: Because quenching mechanism between CDs (CA) or CDs (glucose) and HSA was dynamic quenching, it is collisional quenching that does not exist in the site-selective binding. CDs (AA) site-selective binding with HSA was studied. Warfarin (site I of serum albumin) and ibuprofen (site II of serum albumin) are well known binding site probe [21]. As shown in Fig. 8S(A), in fluorescence spectra of HSA, maximum emission wavelength occur apparent red shift, fluorescence intensity also do not slight decreased with the warfarin addition. Fig. 8S(B) shown when ibuprofen presence, maximum emission wavelength of HSA do not significant decreased and vary trend was same with CDs (AA) addition in blank HSA. The corresponding result were exhibited in Table 4 , it is found that binding constant was significantly change when warfarin presence, however, a slight influence when ibuprofen presence. To sum up, binding site of CDs (AA) was mainly present site I of HSA.

Molecule docking is popular method to acquire more messages about the interaction mechanisms between nanoparticles molecule and biomacromolecules. Through molecule docking can know the binding site, interaction force and name of residues interaction with nanoparticles or drug [40]. The possible molecular structure of CDs (AA) shown in Fig. 5 , it is according to the molecular weight, XPS and FITR analyze obtain function group to design. Crystal structure of HSA can be acquired from the protein Data Bank (PDB ID: 1H9Z). Fig. 6A shown that the CDs (AA) bind with HSA principally on site I that is corresponding with the consequence of site marker competitive experiment. Fig. 6B exhibited that part map of residues interaction with CDs (AA) on site I and red part represent the hydrogen bonds. Fig. 6C illustrate that ARG222, ARG218, LYS195, LYS199, GLN196, HIS242 and ARG257 provide donor atoms for generation hydrogen bonds in activity pocket of site I, and the hydrogen bonds were with the yellow imaginary line. As shown in Fig. 6D, CDs (AA) was at the bind pocket of HSA, it is also show the hydrogen bonds that the yellow imaginary line. The result of molecule docking was corresponded with experiment that the binding site of CDs (AA) may major present site I of HSA.

Cyclic voltammetry and electrochemical impedance are a prevalent method to study about interaction of HSA with CDs [21,41]. As shown in Fig. 7A, the peak current of both redox peaks decreased and the peak potential difference increased with increasing concentrations of HSA, which indicates the decrease of the electron-transfer rate and the conductivity of CDs (CA)/Au electrode. EIS of CDs (CA)/Au electrode shown in Fig. 7B, with increasing concentrations of HSA and the diameter of the semicircle, it is illustrated that add of HSA hinders electron-transfer of electrode surface. Those phenomena suggest HSA interaction with CDs (CA). Fig. 7(CF) show that the various trend Au electrode embellish of HSA with addition CDs (glucose) or CDs (AA). As adding to CDs (glucose) or CDs (AA), the peak current of both redox peaks decreased, the peak potential difference increased, and the diameter of the semicircle increased, which indicates CDs (glucose) or CDs (AA) interaction with HSA. Because quenching mechanism of HSA-CDs (AA) system was mainly static quenching, Langmuir equation used to calculate the binding constant of HSA-CDs (AA) system [15]:
(9)
1
Δ
I P
=
1
Δ
I P
+
1
Δ
I
P max
K a
c
where ∆I p, ∆I pmax, K a and c represents the peak current, maximum of the peak current, binding constant of HSA-CDs (AA) system and concentration of CDs (AA), respectively. From inspection of the insert of Fig. 7E, the value of binding constant Ka HSA-CDs (AA) system was 2.59 × 105 L·mol−1.

The binding constant Ka of EIS was calculated according to the Eq. [15]:
(10)
R ct
i
R ct
=
K a
c + 1
where R ct and R ct (i) represent electron-transfer resistance of the pure HSA and electron-transfer resistance with CDs (AA) adding, respectively. K a stands for binding constant of HSA-CDs (AA) system, c is the concentration of CDs (AA). The insert of Fig. 7F shows, that the binding constant of HSA-CDs (AA) system was 0.16 × 105 L·mol−1. Binding constant of HSA-CDs (AA) system for CV and EIS varies quantitative fluorescent experiment, it may be due to the sensitivity of electrochemical and fluorescent method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conformational changes of HSA with the presence of different N,S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: A typical technical that synchronous fluorescence spectroscopy always study molecular microenvironment vary in the vicinity of fluorophores, it can be estimated the change of polarity around the fluorophores through the condition shift of maximum emission wavelength of fluorophores [42]. In this study, stabilized the D-value Δλ (between excitation wavelength and emission wavelength of fluorophores) was 60 nm indicating microenvironment message of tryptophan residues. Fig. 8A exhibited that maximum emission wavelength of tryptophan was slight red shift (from 345.5 nm to 346.5 nm) with the concentration increased of CDs (CA). With addition CDs (glucose) caused red shift for tryptophan (from 346.0 nm to 347.5 nm) shown in Fig. 8B. As shown in Fig. 8C, increase CDs (AA) concentration accompanies a significant red shift (from 345.5 nm to 347.5 nm) for tryptophan. In conclusion, three different N,S-CDs can make polarity around strengthened of HSA and hydrophilicity of HSA increased.

Circular dichroism (CD) spectrum is an effective method to research the secondary structure changes of proteins. Two typical negative minima of α-helix of HSA are around 208 nm and 222 nm, negative peaks at approximately 208 nm or 222 nm are both attribute to n-π* transfer for the peptide bond of α-helix of HSA [43]. Fig. 8(D–F) shows that when three different N,S-CDs add to a solution of HSA caused significantly reduced in the negative ellipticity of HSA at 208 nm and 222 nm, which indicated that three different N,S-CDs may disturb the stability peptide bond of α-helix of HSA and lead to partial unfolding, so that HSA hydrophilicity was increased.

FTIR spectroscopy is widespread used to research the conformational change of serum albumins [17]. In FTIR spectroscopy, the secondary structure of protein divide into amide I band and amide II band. The amide I appears in the region of 1600–1700 cm−1 and amide II appears in the region of 1500–1600 cm−1 [44]. Fig. 9S shows the FTIR spectra of pure HSA with three different N,S-CDs respectively adding to HSA system, 1609 cm−1 and 1513 cm−1 represent the amide I band and amide II band of free HSA, respectively. With addition the three different N,S-CDs, the amide I band and amide II band of HSA have caused obvious change, suggesting the conformational variety of HSA that in agreement well the fluorescence and CD experiments.

Three-dimensional (3D) fluorescence spectroscopy is an important measurement method which is used to research the conformational changes of proteins. Conformation of HSA affected by different carbon dots are shown in the Fig. 9 , Peak a stand for the Rayleigh scattering peak of HSA (λem = λex), Peak 1 may indicate the spectral behavior change of tryptophan and tyrosine residues of HSA [15]. Peak 2 may be represented the N,S-CDs. The relevant parameters about peak position, stokes shift and fluorescence intensity with different N,S-CDs added to the HSA were exhibited in Table 5 . From this table, fluorescence intensity of HSA had decreased with three different carbon dots added and accompanied with slight red shift, which indicated polar environment of tryptophan and tyrosine residues were changed because three different carbon dots interaction with HSA. Through the 3D fluorescence spectroscopy, the conformational of HSA can be changed by the three different carbon dots.

In this study, high QY of N,S-CDs was prepared via using citric acid as carbon source, and other two different N,S-CDs were produced via vary carbon source. The discrepancy between three different N,S-CDs were discussed, for example, particle diameters, lattice parameters, distinction emission wavelength, et al. When three different N,S-CDs interaction with HSA respectively shown different quenching mechanism, the quenching mechanism between CDs (AA) with HSA is static quenching, the quenching mechanism between CDs (glucose) or CDs (CA) with HSA are dynamic quenching, that the thermodynamic and kinetic are different. And the molecular docking is applied to study the CDs interaction with HSA at first. The three different N,S-CDs can make the conformational of HSA changed. To sum up, those result may make people to understand N,S-CDs interaction with HSA in molecule level, urge the N,S-CDs for the carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials in vivo nanomedical applications.

Chun-Yan Liang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing-original draft. Juan Pan: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Software, Validation. Ai-Min Bai: Resources, Supervision. Yan-Jun Hu: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing-review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21273065), and the Science and Technology Foundation for Excellent Creative Research Group of Hubei Provincial Department of Education (T201810).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2020.01.238.
